paintings.title: Jahangir Visiting the Ascetic Jadrup
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: The emperor Jahangir sits in quiet conversation with the Hindu ascetic Jadrup outside his hermit's cell — an encounter the emperor himself recorded, and one of the most famous images of the meeting of imperial power and renunciation in Mughal art. Govardhan, a Hindu painter whose father Bhavani Das had been a minor painter in the imperial workshop, was renowned for depicting the different human physiques of Indians with much accuracy.
funFacts: Govardhan served three emperors, joining the imperial service under Akbar and continuing through the reigns of Jahangir and Shah Jahan.
An excellent portrait of Govardhan himself was painted by his contemporary Daulat.

paintings.title: Timur Handing the Crown to Babur (Minto Album)
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: In an impossible, dynastic dream-scene from the Minto Album, the world-conqueror Timur hands the imperial crown across generations to Babur, founder of the Mughal empire, in the presence of Babur's son Humayun. Painted for Shah Jahan around 1630, the allegory asserts the unbroken legitimacy of the house of Timur. Govardhan renders the three sovereigns with the rich, sensuous colour and softly modelled forms for which his surviving work is known.
funFacts: Like many other Mughal painters, Govardhan and his family were Hindus working on the Muslim dynasty's grandest commissions.
His portraits from the Jahangir albums are now dispersed among American and European museum collections.

paintings.title: Shah Jahan and Dara Shikoh
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: The emperor Shah Jahan and his favourite son and heir-apparent Dara Shikoh appear together in this double portrait from the height of Govardhan's career in the imperial atelier. Govardhan's extant works show his fondness for rich, sensuous colour and softly modelled forms, qualities that made him one of the most admired portraitists of the Shah Jahan court.
funFacts: Govardhan's career spanned roughly 1595 to 1640, nearly half a century of Mughal painting's golden age.
Dara Shikoh, painted here as heir, would later be defeated and executed by his brother Aurangzeb.

paintings.title: Akbar with Lion and Calf
paintings.medium: ink, opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.location: Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
paintings.story: Painted decades after Akbar's death, this posthumous allegorical portrait shows the great emperor haloed, with a lion and a calf resting together at his feet — an image of the universal peace his reign was remembered for. It was made for the Shah Jahan Album around 1630, when Govardhan was among the senior masters of the imperial workshop he had joined as a young man in Akbar's own service.
funFacts: Govardhan is the subject of an entry in the Encyclopaedia of Islam by the Mughal painting scholar Milo C. Beach.
The lion lying down with the calf echoes the ideal of sulh-i kul, the universal tolerance associated with Akbar's rule.

paintings.title: A Discourse Between Muslim Sages
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.location: Los Angeles County Museum of Art
paintings.story: Two venerable sages sit in discourse, their lined faces and worn bodies studied with the unflinching naturalism that distinguishes Govardhan's paintings of holy men. He depicted the different human physiques of the Indians with much accuracy, and his sympathy for ascetics, dervishes and sages produced some of the most humane images in all Mughal painting.
funFacts: Govardhan's father Bhavani Das was a minor painter in the imperial workshop — the son far outstripped the father's rank.
Paintings of sufis and sages were prized by Jahangir and Shah Jahan alongside formal court portraiture.

paintings.title: Dalliance on a Terrace
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.location: Los Angeles County Museum of Art
paintings.story: A prince and his beloved embrace on a moonlit terrace, attended by musicians — a lyrical night-scene from Govardhan's years in Jahangir's atelier. The painting's rich, sensuous colour and softly modelled forms are exactly the qualities that the surviving record of his work is noted for, here turned from the durbar to the pleasures of the court at ease.
funFacts: Govardhan's significant creations include Jahangir celebrating the festival of Ab-Pashi (1615), kept in the Raza Library at Rampur, India.
He was one of the illustrators of the Baburnama now in the British Museum.

paintings.title: Khan 'Alam with a Sparrow Hawk
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.location: Arthur M. Sackler Museum, Harvard Art Museums
paintings.story: Khan 'Alam, Jahangir's falconer and his ambassador to Iran, stands with a sparrow hawk on his gloved hand in a portrait now at the Harvard Art Museums. Govardhan's portraits, many of them contained in the Jahangir albums, are today spread through American and European collections, records of the officials, envoys and holy men of the empire painted with his characteristic accuracy.
funFacts: Khan 'Alam led Jahangir's embassy to the court of Shah Abbas of Persia, the same exchange of gifts that brought exotic animals for Mansur to paint.
Govardhan's work bridges the reigns of Akbar, Jahangir and Shah Jahan stylistically as well as chronologically.

paintings.title: Shaikh Hosein Jami and Attendant
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: The venerable Shaikh Hosein Jami leans on his staff beside a young attendant in this signed work from the early 1620s, now in the Musée Guimet. Signed paintings are precious anchors for reconstructing Govardhan's career, which the sources place between about 1595 and 1640, and his studies of religious men rank among the most penetrating character portraits of the Mughal school.
funFacts: The Musée Guimet in Paris holds several of Govardhan's finest works, including Jahangir Visiting the Ascetic Jadrup.
Govardhan's Astrologer and Holy Men, from the Late Shah Jahan Album of c. 1650, shows his manner carried to the end of his school's era.
